chunks.text: Two changes are needed. First, add a `category` field to the `Task` node:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Then update `add_task` to call the AI:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: `str(categorize(title)).split(".")[-1].lower()` converts `Category.SHOPPING` to `"shopping"` for clean display. Notice the return type is still `-> Task` -- the new `category` field is automatically included when the typed object crosses the client-server boundary. The other endpoints (`get_tasks`, `toggle_task`, `delete_task`) don't need any changes either, because they already return typed `Task` objects that now include the `category` field automatically.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **Structured Output with obj and sem**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Now for a more advanced use case: the shopping list. The `categorize` function returns a single enum value -- simple. But what if you need the AI to return *structured* data -- not just a string or a category, but a list of ingredients, each with a name, quantity, unit, and estimated cost? This is where `obj` and `sem` come together.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **`obj`** defines a structured data type that serves as an output schema for the LLM. Unlike `node`, objects aren't stored in the graph -- they're data containers that describe the *shape* of what the AI should return:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **`sem`** adds a semantic hint that tells the LLM what an ambiguous field means:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Consider why this matters: without `sem`, `cost: float` is ambiguous to the LLM -- cost in what currency? Per unit or total? Per serving? With the semantic hint, the LLM knows exactly what to generate. This is a general principle: **the more precise your types and hints, the more reliable the AI output**.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Now the AI function:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: The LLM returns a `list[Ingredient]` -- a list of typed objects, each with name, quantity, unit, cost, and carb flag. Jac validates the structure automatically, ensuring every field has the correct type. If the LLM produces malformed output, the runtime catches it rather than letting bad data propagate through your application.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **Shopping List Nodes and Endpoints**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Now you need to persist the AI-generated ingredients in the graph. Notice that `Ingredient` (an `obj`) is used for AI output, while `ShoppingItem` (a `node`) is used for persistence. This separation is intentional -- the AI schema and the storage schema can evolve independently:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: And three new endpoints:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Compare these endpoints to the task endpoints -- the same pattern applies. Instead of manually constructing dictionaries with every field, you return typed objects directly. The runtime serializes all `has` fields automatically when they cross the client-server boundary. `generate_list` returns `list[ShoppingItem]` and `get_shopping_list` does the same -- no manual dict construction needed.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Notice how `generate_list` clears old shopping items before generating new ones -- this ensures you always see a fresh list. The graph now holds both task and shopping data, demonstrating how different types of nodes coexist naturally:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text:
```mermaid
graph LR
    root((root)) --> T1["Task(#quot;Buy groceries#quot;, shopping)"]
    root --> T2["Task(#quot;Team standup#quot;, work)"]
    root --> S1["ShoppingItem(#quot;Chicken breast#quot;, 2lb, $5.99)"]:::shopping
    root --> S2["ShoppingItem(#quot;Soy sauce#quot;, 2tbsp, $0.50)"]:::shopping
    classDef shopping stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **Update the Frontend**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: The frontend needs a two-column layout: tasks on the left, shopping list on the right. Update the component with new state, methods, and the shopping panel:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **Componentizing the UI**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: The two-column `app` function works, but look at the code: it's mixing four different concerns -- task list state, shopping list state, the task row markup, and the ingredient row markup. As any UI grows, this single-component approach becomes harder to navigate and harder to change. The fix is the same idea that React, Vue, and Svelte use: **break the UI into smaller, focused components**.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: In Jac, a component is just a `cl def:pub` function that returns `JsxElement`. Components can take parameters (called *props*), own their own `has` state, and compose into bigger components. You'll factor the app into four:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text:
| Component | Owns state? | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| `TaskItem` | No | Renders one task row given a `task` and callbacks |
| `TasksColumn` | Yes (tasks, input text) | Owns the task list and fetches on mount |
| `IngredientItem` | No | Renders one ingredient row |
| `ShoppingColumn` | Yes (ingredients, meal text) | Owns the shopping list and fetches on mount |

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: The top-level `app` then becomes a trivial composition of `<TasksColumn/>` and `<ShoppingColumn/>` -- no shared state, no mixed concerns.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **A presentational component: `TaskItem`**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: A component that takes props but owns no state is called *presentational* -- it renders what it's given, and the parent decides what happens on interaction:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Two things to notice. First, `task: Task` is a real typed `node` -- the same type used on the server. Because `Task` returned by `get_tasks()` crosses the client-server boundary as a typed object, `TaskItem` works on the original typed instance, not a dict. Second, `onToggle: Callable[[], None]` declares that the parent must pass a function taking no arguments and returning nothing. `Callable` is how Jac describes function signatures in type annotations.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **A stateful component: `TasksColumn`**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: `TasksColumn` owns the task list and the input text. It fetches on mount, makes server calls in response to user actions, and renders `TaskItem` instances:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: The rendering is much easier to read now: a header, an input row, a list of `<TaskItem/>` elements, and a count. The inline lambdas for `onToggle` and `onDelete` close over `jid(t)` so each row knows which task it's acting on. This pattern -- passing callbacks that capture per-row data -- is how parent components stay in control of what happens when a child is interacted with.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **The shopping components**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: `IngredientItem` and `ShoppingColumn` follow the same shape -- presentational row, then stateful column:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI
Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: **The new `app`**

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: With the column components doing all the heavy lifting, `app` is now small enough to read in a breath:

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text:
```jac cl def:pub app -> JsxElement { return

AI Day Planner

; } ```

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Step back and notice the architectural payoff. The two columns are *independent* -- each owns its own state, makes its own server calls, and renders itself. Adding a third column, swapping `<TasksColumn/>` for a routing wrapper, or reusing `<TaskItem/>` somewhere else is now a local change that doesn't touch the rest of the app. This is the same separation-of-concerns thinking that makes graphs and `def:priv` clean -- only applied to UI.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text:
!!! info "Components live in one file -- for now"
    All four components currently live in the same `main.jac`. That's fine for a tutorial. In a real project, each component would live in its own file -- you'll see that layout in Part 6, where you'll split components into a `components/` folder.

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

meta.section: Part 5: Making It Smart with AI

chunks.text: Replace `styles.css` with the expanded version that supports the two-column layout and shopping list:
